# T33: Negative-Evidence Predicates Established

Edition: both. This fixture is the reverse-direction partner to t06.

## Input

> Lydia Frost was documented as an unmarried resident of Oak County from 1840
> through 1860, with no evidence she left. The county's original marriage
> registers survive and require an entry for a legal marriage. I searched every
> page of the original registers, the index, known name variants, and the
> neighboring counties for 1840-1860. No entry appears. Does this prove she
> never married?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Classify the absence as meaningful Negative Evidence for no recorded legal
  marriage in Oak County or its searched neighbors from 1840-1860
- [ ] Identify the established predicates: expected residence, coverage dates,
  surviving original registers, exhaustive variant search, and adjacent
  jurisdictions
- [ ] Explain the limit: this evidence does not prove Lydia never married at
  any place or time, or that an unrecorded marriage never occurred
- [ ] State what additional evidence would be needed to extend the conclusion

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT conclude that Lydia Frost never married anywhere or at any time
- [ ] Must NOT generalize this result to every failed search
- [ ] Must NOT invent a marriage, repository holding, or record-loss event

## Ground Truth

The established predicates make this absence meaningful Negative Evidence
against a recorded legal marriage in the specified counties and years. Its
scope is limited: it cannot establish that Lydia never married elsewhere, after
1860, or outside the legal-record system. Additional residence, later-life,
religious, and family evidence would be needed to extend the conclusion.
